questions.stem: You are writing a JGiven scenario and need a stage that builds the starting context (a list of ingredients) for later stages. Which kind of stage are you authoring?

options.text: An assertion-only stage with no fields

questions.explanation: The starting context is the Given step, so you write a Given stage (e.g. GivenIngredients) that produces the context. When does the action, Then asserts. (BDD p.11)

questions.stem: You are coding the When stage and must mark the field that holds the value this stage creates for the Then stage to check. Which annotation do you place on it?
questions.explanation: The When stage produces a value for downstream stages, so it is @ProvidedScenarioState (producer). @ExpectedScenarioState is the consumer side and @Autowired is Spring DI. (BDD p.15)

questions.stem: While writing a Then stage, you need to read a value the When stage already produced. Which annotation do you put on the field?
questions.explanation: A Then stage consumes upstream values, so the field is @ExpectedScenarioState (consumer). @ProvidedScenarioState would mark it as something this stage produces, which is wrong for a consumer. (BDD p.16)

questions.stem: You are turning a behaviour into a JGiven test method and want the body to read like Given/When/Then. Which calls do you chain?
questions.explanation: JGiven expresses GWT directly with given()/when()/then() and and() chained inside a JUnit method. The other triads are unit-test lifecycle or AAA naming, not JGiven. (BDD p.10)

questions.stem: You are role-playing the cook: your When stage needs the actual Cook object injected by Spring AND wants that object shared as scenario state. Which annotation combination matches the deck's WhenCook?

options.text: @Autowired together with @ScenarioState on the Cook field
@Autowired alone, never combined with a state annotation

questions.explanation: WhenCook declares the Cook with both @Autowired (Spring injects it) and @ScenarioState (it is shared bidirectionally). Provided/Expected alone would not show the Spring integration the deck demonstrates. (BDD p.15)

questions.stem: You want your new step method to keep the fluent chain alive so the next .and() call compiles. What must the method do?

options.text: Return this (the stage), enabled by Stage<SELF>
Return a fresh new stage instance each call

questions.explanation: Fluent chaining needs each step to 'return this', which the Stage<SELF> self-type makes type-safe. void breaks the chain, throwing is wrong, and returning a new instance would lose accumulated state. (BDD p.14)

questions.stem: You are writing the final Then step that only asserts and has nothing chained after it. Is a void return type acceptable?

options.text: Only if you also remove the @ExpectedScenarioState field
No — every JGiven step must return the stage
Yes — nothing chains after a Then step, so no self return is needed
No — Then steps must return a boolean pass/fail

questions.explanation: Then steps like ThenMeal's may be void because no further .and() chains after them; the self return is only needed mid-chain. (BDD p.16)

questions.stem: You are mapping a user story to a scenario for the lab portfolio. What is the FIRST artefact you must produce from each story?

options.text: The production code that implements it

questions.explanation: The lab's required first move is to write each user story's Given/When/Then before automating it with JGiven + AssertJ. Production code and diagrams come later; JGiven needs no .feature file. (BDDLab p.1)

questions.stem: You are authoring a Given stage method an_egg() that should delegate to the_ingredient("egg") and stay chainable. What should its body do?
questions.explanation: In GivenIngredients, an_egg() is implemented as 'return the_ingredient("egg");' so it both records the ingredient and returns the stage for chaining. A bare add with no return breaks the fluent chain. (BDD p.14)

questions.stem: You are writing a When stage step and want a quick sanity guard that the injected collaborator is present before acting. Which library does the deck use right inside the When stage for this?
questions.explanation: WhenCook uses AssertJ's assertThat(cook).isNotNull() as a sanity check inside the When step, foreshadowing the AssertJ half of the deck. Hamcrest/JUnit/Mockito are not what the slide shows. (BDD p.15)

questions.stem: You are writing a scenario whose Then step should read like domain vocabulary, e.g. assertThat(student).isInMiddleSchool(). Which authoring step do you take in AssertJ?

options.text: Write a Gherkin step definition file
Subclass AbstractAssert (e.g. StudentAssert) and add isInMiddleSchool()
Add an isInMiddleSchool() method to java.lang.String

questions.explanation: Custom assertions are made by subclassing AbstractAssert to add domain methods, then subclassing Assertions for the factory. Editing String or using Gherkin/equals does not create a fluent custom assertion. (BDD p.28)

questions.stem: You role-play verifying a JHotDraw change: 'Given a drawing with one rectangle, When the user drags it 50px right, Then x increases by 50.' Which tool drives the actual drag on the Swing canvas?
questions.explanation: Driving real GUI interaction (drag 'n drop) on a Swing app like JHotDraw is AssertJ-Swing's job. JUnit/Hamcrest only assert values; Cucumber parses plain text, it does not drive a GUI. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: You need the When stage to expose two produced values, dough and meal, to the Then stage. How should both fields be annotated?

options.text: @ScenarioState is forbidden so leave them unannotated

questions.explanation: Both dough and meal are produced by WhenCook for later use, so each carries @ProvidedScenarioState. Expected would mark them as consumed, and unannotated fields are not transferred. (BDD p.15)

questions.stem: You are reusing a GivenADrawing stage across many JHotDraw figure scenarios. Which JGiven property are you exploiting?

options.text: Stage classes give modularity and reuse

questions.explanation: Reusing one stage across many scenarios is the modularity/reuse benefit of JGiven stage classes. Gherkin inheritance, Spring profiles and Maven modules are unrelated mechanisms. (BDD p.11)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of Given/When/Then's three keywords?
questions.explanation: GWT consists of Given, When and Then (chained with And). 'Where' is not a scenario keyword. (BDD p.5)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT a property of BDD as defined by the deck?

options.text: Experts and developers collaborate to define it
Behaviour is in a common domain language
It is executed like normal tests
Behaviour is hidden from domain experts

questions.explanation: BDD deliberately makes behaviour readable by domain experts; 'hidden from experts' is the opposite. Domain language, collaboration and being executed like tests are all real BDD properties. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: Which item is NOT among the deck's 'typical test issues' that motivate BDD?

options.text: Tests are too readable by domain experts
The point of the test is hard to grasp

questions.explanation: Being readable by domain experts is the goal, not a problem. Irrelevant details, duplication and unclear purpose are the listed issues. (BDD p.3)

questions.stem: Which framework is NOT in the deck's 'classical plain-text' category?
questions.explanation: JGiven is the deck's developer-friendly (pure-Java) framework, not a classical plain-text one. Cucumber, JBehave and Concordion are all classical. (BDD p.6-7)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a developer-friendly framework listed by the deck?
questions.explanation: Fitnesse is a classical Wiki+Java framework, not developer-friendly. Spock, ScalaTest and Serenity appear on the developer-friendly slide. (BDD p.6-7)

questions.stem: Which is NOT something a domain expert can do with JGiven?

options.text: Use the report as living documentation

questions.explanation: JGiven's key limitation: domain experts CANNOT author scenarios (those are Java); they can only read the reports. The other three are all things experts can do. (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: A teammate lists four 'JGiven wins' to put on a slide. Which one must be removed because it is actually the framework's limitation?
questions.explanation: Expert authoring is JGiven's limitation, not a win — experts can only read reports. JUnit/TestNG integration, Apache 2 and CI plugins are genuine strengths. (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: Which library is NOT one the deck says developers confusingly mixed before adopting AssertJ?
questions.explanation: The deck cites 'a confusion of JUnit, Hamcrest and Fest'. Mockito is a mocking library and is not part of that assertion-confusion list. (BDD p.22)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a capability the deck attributes to AssertJ-Swing?

options.text: Compiling Swing apps to native code

questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing simulates interaction, looks up components, and embeds failure screenshots; it does not compile apps to native code. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: Which statement about classical frameworks is NOT supported by the deck?

options.text: They carry an additional maintenance cost
They keep scenarios in a separate plain-text file
They eliminate the need for step-definition glue

questions.explanation: Classical frameworks REQUIRE step-definition glue between the plain-text file and Java — they do not eliminate it. The other three statements are accurate. (BDD p.6)

questions.stem: Which annotation is NOT part of JGiven's state-transfer trio?
questions.explanation: The trio is @ScenarioState, @ProvidedScenarioState, @ExpectedScenarioState. '@ConsumedScenarioState' does not exist in JGiven (consumption is expressed by @ExpectedScenarioState). (BDD p.13)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a report format JGiven produces from a run?
questions.explanation: JGiven generates console and HTML5 reports (living documentation). A printed PDF brochure is not a format the deck mentions. (BDD p.17-18)

questions.stem: Which claim about stage classes is NOT true per the deck?

options.text: Typically one is used per Given/When/Then step
They are a feature shared by Cucumber and JBehave

questions.explanation: Stage classes are unique to JGiven, so claiming Cucumber/JBehave share them is false. Modularity, uniqueness and one-per-step are all correct. (BDD p.11)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a reason the deck gives for preferring AssertJ over the alternatives?

options.text: It is the only library bundled inside the JDK

questions.explanation: AssertJ is a third-party library, not bundled in the JDK. The deck praises it for being maintained, a near-superset, and readable. (BDD p.23)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a way AssertJ-Swing locates GUI components?
questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing looks up components by type, by name, or by custom criteria — not by raw memory address. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: What primarily distinguishes classical Cucumber from developer-friendly JGiven?

options.text: Cucumber produces no reports while JGiven does
Cucumber keeps scenarios in separate plain-text files; JGiven keeps them in Java
Cucumber runs on CI but JGiven cannot
Cucumber is Java-only while JGiven is plain-text-only

questions.explanation: The core contrast is artefact location: Cucumber's plain-text .feature + glue versus JGiven's pure-Java scenarios. Both run on CI and both report; the Java/plain-text claim is reversed. (BDD p.6-7)

questions.stem: Regarding who can AUTHOR scenarios, how do plain-text Cucumber and JGiven differ?

options.text: Neither lets anyone but a compiler author scenarios
Only JGiven lets experts author scenarios
A domain expert could in principle write a Cucumber .feature file, but not a JGiven scenario
Both equally let experts author scenarios

questions.explanation: Plain-text Gherkin is in principle authorable by a non-developer; JGiven scenarios are Java and experts cannot author them — that is JGiven's trade-off. (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: How is BDD usually contrasted with TDD in the guide?

options.text: BDD is for GUIs only; TDD is for back ends only
They are identical with different names
BDD abandons test-first entirely; TDD keeps it
BDD reframes test-first around externally observable behaviour in domain language; TDD drives design with developer-facing unit tests

questions.explanation: BDD keeps TDD's test-first discipline but shifts focus to observable behaviour in ubiquitous language; it is 'TDD done with the customer's vocabulary', not its abandonment. (BDD p.5)

questions.stem: Compared with Hamcrest, how does the deck characterise AssertJ?

options.text: AssertJ is actively maintained and readable; Hamcrest is stagnant and ugly
AssertJ is a subset of Hamcrest
AssertJ is abandonware; Hamcrest is the modern choice
Both are abandoned and should be avoided

questions.explanation: The deck calls Hamcrest stagnant/ugly and Fest abandonware, recommending AssertJ as maintained, readable, and a near-superset. AssertJ is not a subset. (BDD p.22-23)

questions.stem: Contrast the report-text source of classical Cucumber versus JGiven.

options.text: JGiven parses a .feature file; Cucumber uses method names
Cucumber parses a plain-text .feature file; JGiven derives text from class/method/step names

questions.explanation: Cucumber reads the plain-text scenario; JGiven generates report prose from the Java test class, method and snake_case step names — no .feature file. (BDD p.10,17)

questions.stem: Both @ProvidedScenarioState and @ExpectedScenarioState mark scenario-state fields. How do they differ?

options.text: @Provided is for Spring beans; @Expected is for primitives
@Provided is the producing (out) side; @Expected is the consuming (in) side

questions.explanation: Provided = producer (writes out), Expected = consumer (reads in). They are directional opposites, not synonyms, and neither is tied to Spring vs primitives. (BDD p.13)

questions.stem: How does @ScenarioState differ from the directional pair?

options.text: @ScenarioState is write-only; the pair is read-only
@ScenarioState is deprecated in favour of the pair
@ScenarioState is bidirectional (read and written); the pair is one-directional each

questions.explanation: @ScenarioState transfers values both ways, whereas @Provided (out) and @Expected (in) are each one-directional. It is not write-only, Then-only, or deprecated. (BDD p.13)

questions.stem: Compare AssertJ's and JUnit's native assertions as the deck frames them.

options.text: JUnit's are simplistic/underpowered; AssertJ is fluent and a near-superset
JUnit's are more powerful and fluent than AssertJ
AssertJ lacks type-specific assertions that JUnit has

questions.explanation: The deck says JUnit assertions were underpowered from the start, motivating AssertJ's fluent, polymorphic, near-superset API. JUnit is not more fluent, nor does AssertJ lack type-specific asserts. (BDD p.22-24)

questions.stem: Contrast a JGiven Condition functor with an AssertJ custom assertion.

options.text: A Condition is a reusable matches() predicate used via is()/isNot(); a custom assertion is a domain method on an AbstractAssert subclass
Custom assertions cannot be reused but Conditions can never be reused
A Condition subclasses AbstractAssert; a custom assertion overrides matches()

questions.explanation: A Condition overrides matches() and is applied with is()/isNot(); a custom assertion adds a named domain method by subclassing AbstractAssert. They are distinct extension points. (BDD p.27-28)

questions.stem: Contrast where AssertJ and JGiven operate in a BDD scenario.

options.text: AssertJ structures the scenario; JGiven does the assertions
JGiven structures the GWT scenario; AssertJ performs the assertions inside Then

questions.explanation: JGiven provides the GWT scaffolding and stages; AssertJ is the fluent assertion engine used inside Then steps (and as sanity checks). They are complementary. (BDD p.16,24)

questions.stem: How does the deck contrast Fest with AssertJ?

options.text: Fest is the maintained successor to AssertJ
Fest is a superset of AssertJ
Fest looks to be abandonware; AssertJ is actively maintained

questions.explanation: The deck calls Fest abandonware and recommends AssertJ as the maintained near-superset. AssertJ, not Fest, is the active project. (BDD p.22-23)

questions.stem: Contrast the maintenance profile of classical versus developer-friendly frameworks, per the deck's verdict.

options.text: Classical add maintenance cost (two synced artefacts); developer-friendly keep one artefact in the production language
Classical have one artefact; developer-friendly have two
Developer-friendly add maintenance cost; classical remove it

questions.explanation: Classical frameworks carry 'Additional Maintenance Cost' from the plain-text file plus glue; developer-friendly frameworks remove the second artefact. The reversed options are wrong. (BDD p.6-7)

questions.stem: A field reads: '@ProvidedScenarioState List<String> ingredients = new ArrayList<>();' inside a class extending Stage. Which stage is this most likely from?
questions.explanation: A produced ingredients list is built by the Given stage GivenIngredients. The When stage produces dough/meal, Then consumes meal, and an Assertions subclass is not a JGiven stage. (BDD p.14)

questions.stem: A stage declares: '@ExpectedScenarioState String meal;' and asserts assertThat(meal).isEqualTo("pancake"). Which stage class is this?
questions.explanation: Consuming meal and asserting it is the Then stage ThenMeal. Given produces ingredients, When produces meal, and a Condition is not a stage. (BDD p.16)

questions.stem: A class has '@Autowired @ScenarioState Cook cook;', '@ExpectedScenarioState List<String> ingredients;', and '@ProvidedScenarioState String meal;'. Which stage is it?
questions.explanation: Consuming ingredients, using an injected Cook, and producing meal identifies the When stage WhenCook. Given only provides, Then only consumes, Assertions is AssertJ. (BDD p.15)

questions.stem: A snippet reads: 'assertThat(actual).contains("is").startsWith("This");'. Which library is this?
questions.explanation: Chained fluent assertions like contains().startsWith() are AssertJ's signature style. Hamcrest uses assertThat(x, matcher), JUnit uses assertEquals, and Mockito mocks. (BDD p.25)

questions.stem: A snippet defines 'new Condition<Integer>(){ public boolean matches(Integer v){ return v%6==0; } }' and uses assertThat(12).is(...). Which AssertJ feature is shown?
questions.explanation: Overriding matches() and applying it with is()/isNot() is the custom Condition functor. A custom assertion subclasses AbstractAssert; this snippet does neither that nor Swing lookup. (BDD p.27)

questions.stem: A method body is: 'given().an_egg().and().some_milk().and().the_ingredient("flour");'. Which clause of the scenario is this?
questions.explanation: given()...and()... establishes the pre-conditions, i.e. the Given clause. when() would be the action and then() the assertion. (BDD p.10)

questions.stem: A line reads 'when().the_cook_mangles_everything_to_a_dough().and().the_cook_fries_the_dough_in_a_pan();'. Which clause is it?

options.text: The When (the action that exercises behaviour)

questions.explanation: Calls under when() are the action steps — the When clause. Context is given(), outcome is then(), and this is method-body code, not a class declaration. (BDD p.10)

questions.stem: A class extends 'Stage<GivenIngredients>'. What does the generic parameter (the self-type) enable?
questions.explanation: Stage<SELF> is the self-type that makes 'return this' return the concrete stage type for fluent chaining. It is unrelated to DI, reports, or threading. (BDD p.11,14)

questions.stem: A test imports 'static org.assertj.core.api.Assertions.assertThat;'. Which library and purpose does this indicate?

options.text: AssertJ — to use the fluent assertThat() factory in Then steps

questions.explanation: The org.assertj.core.api.Assertions.assertThat static import is AssertJ's fluent factory, used in Then steps. It is not Hamcrest, JUnit, or JGiven's stage API. (BDD p.16,24)

questions.stem: A console listing prints 'Given an egg / And some milk / ... / Then the resulting meal is a pan cake'. What produced it?

options.text: JGiven's console report from the executed scenario

questions.explanation: JGiven prints the executed scenario back as Given/And/When/Then prose — its console living documentation. It is generated from the Java run, not a .feature file or UML. (BDD p.17)

questions.stem: Because JGiven scenarios are written in Java rather than a separate .feature file, what is the direct consequence?

options.text: The scenarios can no longer run on CI
Reports can no longer be read by experts
Domain experts can now author scenarios easily
There is no second artefact to keep in sync, lowering maintenance cost

questions.explanation: Living in Java removes the plain-text file plus glue, cutting the classical maintenance cost. It does not enable expert authoring, break CI, or stop experts reading reports. (BDD p.10,20)

questions.stem: Domain experts can read JGiven reports but cannot author the scenarios. What practical implication does the deck draw from this?

options.text: Developers must write the scenarios; collaboration happens via the readable reports

questions.explanation: Since only developers can author JGiven scenarios, collaboration is mediated by the expert-readable reports rather than expert authoring. BDD still works, and reports remain valuable. (BDD p.19-20)

questions.stem: Because a BDD scenario is executable, what follows when the underlying behaviour drifts from the spec?

options.text: The scenario silently goes out of date like prose
The scenario fails, automatically flagging the drift
The drift is undetectable until a manual review

questions.explanation: An executable spec runs, so drift makes it fail — drift is caught automatically, unlike stale prose which silently rots. (BDD p.4,17)

questions.stem: Because classical frameworks keep two artefacts (plain text + step glue) in two languages, what is the consequence?

options.text: No need for any Java code
Extra maintenance cost from keeping them in sync

questions.explanation: Two synced artefacts in two languages create the 'Additional Maintenance Cost' the deck cites. It does not speed execution or remove the Java glue. (BDD p.6)

questions.stem: Because verification that is expensive to maintain tends to be neglected, what does this argue for in BDD tooling?

options.text: Writing scenarios in three languages at once
Choosing low-maintenance developer-friendly frameworks like JGiven

questions.explanation: If costly verification gets neglected, lowering maintenance (developer-friendly JGiven) keeps verification alive. Manual-only or multi-language approaches worsen the problem. (BDD p.6-7)

questions.stem: Because a Then step has nothing chained after it, what is the consequence for its method signature in ThenMeal?

options.text: It may safely be declared void
It must throw a checked exception
It must return a new stage instance

questions.explanation: With nothing to chain, a Then step needs no self return and may be void (as ThenMeal is). It need not return a stage, throw, or be static. (BDD p.16)

questions.stem: Because step methods 'return this', what becomes possible across calls?
questions.explanation: Returning the stage enables the fluent .and() chain. It does not cause parallelism, DI, or .feature generation. (BDD p.14)

questions.stem: Because JHotDraw is a Swing application prone to threading mistakes, why is AssertJ-Swing's EDT support valuable when verifying a change?

options.text: It removes the need for any assertions
It can test violations of Swing's Event Dispatch Thread rules

questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing can detect EDT (threading-rule) violations, important for a GUI framework like JHotDraw. It does not compile, remove assertions, or port to JavaFX. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: Because the Given stage marks ingredients @ProvidedScenarioState, what can the When stage do with that field?

options.text: Only read it if it is static
Ignore it; provided state is never transferred
Modify it only through the database
Consume it by declaring an @ExpectedScenarioState field of the same type

questions.explanation: Provided state flows into a same-typed @ExpectedScenarioState field downstream, so When consumes the ingredients. Transfer is automatic and field-based, not static- or DB-bound. (BDD p.13-15)

questions.stem: Because JGiven ships Maven and Jenkins plugins, what does this enable for the change process?

options.text: Scenarios run automatically in CI, tying Verification to the Conclusion/CI phase

questions.explanation: Maven/Jenkins plugins let scenarios execute automatically on every integration, connecting Verification to CI. They do not force manual runs or change authoring/readability. (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: You need to assert that a String both contains a substring and starts with a prefix in one readable statement. Which is most appropriate?

options.text: A Hamcrest assertThat with a single matcher

questions.explanation: AssertJ's fluent chain reads as one sentence and is the deck's recommended style. Separate assertTrue calls are less readable, and Mockito is for mocks, not assertions. (BDD p.25)

questions.stem: You want a reusable predicate 'is evenly divisible by six' applied to many integer assertions. Which AssertJ feature fits best?

options.text: A custom AbstractAssert subclass per number
A custom Condition used with is()/isNot()

questions.explanation: A reusable predicate is exactly a Condition functor (override matches, apply via is/isNot). A full AbstractAssert subclass is heavier; Swing and Gherkin are irrelevant here. (BDD p.27)

questions.stem: Your domain type Student needs natural assertions like isInMiddleSchool() and isSenior(). Which approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Define a single Condition and reuse it for both checks
Subclass AbstractAssert (StudentAssert) plus Assertions for the factory

questions.explanation: Multiple named domain assertions belong on a custom AbstractAssert subclass with a matching Assertions factory. A lone Condition gives is()/isNot(), not named methods. (BDD p.28)

questions.stem: To verify that dragging a figure on JHotDraw's canvas updates its position, which tool is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Driving and verifying real Swing GUI interaction is AssertJ-Swing's purpose. Plain assertions cannot perform the drag; Cucumber parses text, not GUIs. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: You want low-maintenance BDD where scenarios live in your Java codebase. Which framework best fits?
questions.explanation: JGiven keeps scenarios in pure Java, removing the classical maintenance cost. Cucumber, Fitnesse and Concordion are classical plain-text/markup frameworks. (BDD p.7,20)

questions.stem: You need a single assertion library that is maintained and replaces JUnit/Hamcrest/Fest. Which does the deck recommend?
questions.explanation: AssertJ is the deck's recommendation: maintained, a near-superset, and readable. Hamcrest is stagnant, Fest abandonware, JUnit underpowered. (BDD p.23)

questions.stem: A non-developer stakeholder must be able to READ verified behaviours, but only developers will write tests. Which tool+practice combination is most appropriate?

options.text: Cucumber where experts write every .feature file
Manual test scripts in a spreadsheet
BDD with JGiven (experts read the reports; developers author scenarios)

questions.explanation: JGiven gives expert-readable reports while keeping authoring with developers — matching the stated constraint. Plain JUnit lacks readable reports; the other options misallocate authoring. (BDD p.9,20)

questions.stem: For the lab, which toolset does the brief say to use to automate the BDD scenarios?

options.text: JGiven + AssertJ (and AssertJ-Swing for Swing apps)

questions.explanation: The lab requires JGiven + AssertJ, plus AssertJ-Swing for Swing applications. The other combinations are not the lab's prescribed tools. (BDDLab p.1)

questions.stem: You want the factory call that returns the correct assertion type for whatever value you pass. Which is the right entry point?
questions.explanation: assertThat() is AssertJ's type-specific factory returning the right assert per type. AbstractAssert is a base class you subclass, matches() is a Condition method, and assertEquals is JUnit. (BDD p.24)

questions.stem: A team wants each integration build to automatically run its BDD acceptance scenarios. Which JGiven feature best supports this?

options.text: Its Maven and Jenkins plugins for CI

questions.explanation: Maven/Jenkins plugins integrate JGiven into CI so scenarios run on every build. Naming, colours and licensing do not provide CI execution. (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: Apply the mnemonic: @ProvidedScenarioState = out, @ExpectedScenarioState = in, @ScenarioState = both. Which field correctly uses @ExpectedScenarioState?

options.text: A field that must be both read and written
A Given-stage field that creates fresh state
A Then-stage field that reads a value an earlier stage produced

questions.explanation: Expected = in = consumer, so a Then field reading upstream state is @ExpectedScenarioState. Creating fresh state is @Provided, both-ways is @ScenarioState, and DI is @Autowired. (BDD p.13)

questions.stem: True or false misconception check: 'Domain experts can write JGiven scenarios.' What is correct?

options.text: False — they can read the reports but cannot author JGiven scenarios
True — that is JGiven's main selling point
True — but only in the HTML5 editor
False — they cannot even read the reports

questions.explanation: The deck's classic trap: experts cannot AUTHOR JGiven scenarios (they are Java); they can only READ the generated reports. (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'given(), when(), then() are Gherkin keywords parsed from text.' What is the accurate statement for JGiven?

options.text: They are Java method calls, not parsed plain-text keywords
They are Gherkin keywords in a .feature file

questions.explanation: In JGiven, given()/when()/then() are Java methods expressing GWT in code — there is no parsed .feature file. They are not XML, Gherkin, or JUnit annotations. (BDD p.10)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Classical plain-text frameworks are better because plain text is simpler to maintain.' What is the deck's rebuttal?

options.text: Plain text always runs slower at runtime
Plain text plus step-definition glue must be kept in sync — that is extra maintenance cost
Plain text is illegal under Apache 2

questions.explanation: The deck's verdict is 'Additional Maintenance Cost' from syncing the plain-text file with step glue — the opposite of 'simpler'. Versioning, licensing and runtime speed are not the point. (BDD p.6)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Stage classes exist in Cucumber and JBehave too.' What is correct?

options.text: All BDD frameworks have stage classes
Stage classes are part of the Gherkin spec
Stage classes are a unique feature of JGiven

questions.explanation: The deck states stage classes are unique to JGiven; other BDD frameworks do not have them, and they are not part of Gherkin. (BDD p.11)

questions.stem: A study slide draft says 'pick Hamcrest for Then-step assertions.' Per the deck, what single word best describes Hamcrest's state?
questions.explanation: The deck calls Hamcrest stagnant (and ugly) and steers you to AssertJ instead. It is not cutting-edge, JDK-bundled, or the deck's recommendation. (BDD p.22-23)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'BDD is just another name for Cucumber.' Why is this wrong?

options.text: BDD is a practice (behaviour in domain language, GWT, collaboration, living docs); Cucumber/JGiven are tools that implement it
Cucumber predates the idea of BDD

questions.explanation: BDD is the practice; Cucumber and JGiven are tools. Equating the practice with one tool is the misconception. BDD is not a language. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: Misconception: '@ProvidedScenarioState means the stage expects a value from earlier.' Correct it.

options.text: Provided is only for Spring beans
No — Provided means the stage PRODUCES (outputs) the value; Expected is the one that consumes

questions.explanation: Provided = producer (out); Expected = consumer (in). The misconception swaps them. They are not synonyms or Spring-specific. (BDD p.13)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'A JGiven scenario needs a .feature file like Cucumber.' What is true?

options.text: JGiven needs both a .feature and an XML file
JGiven generates a .feature file before running
No — a JGiven scenario is a Java test method with no separate .feature file

questions.explanation: JGiven scenarios are pure Java test methods; there is no .feature file at all. That absence is the whole maintenance saving. (BDD p.10)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'The BDD deck cites JHotDraw and walks the eight-phase change process.' What is accurate?

options.text: The deck does NOT mention JHotDraw or the eight phases; that link is course-frame synthesis
The deck names all eight phases
The deck devotes a slide to JHotDraw
The deck replaces JHotDraw with Swing examples on every slide

questions.explanation: The BDD deck is tool-focused and does not name JHotDraw or the eight phases; the connection is supplied by the course frame, not a deck slide. (BDD p.1)

questions.stem: Order the @...ScenarioState flow for the pancake pipeline. Which sequence is correct?

options.text: Then provides ingredients -> When expects meal -> Given asserts
Given provides ingredients -> When expects ingredients and provides meal -> Then expects meal
When provides ingredients -> Given expects meal -> Then provides dough
Given expects meal -> When provides ingredients -> Then provides meal

questions.explanation: Ingredients are provided by Given, consumed by When (which provides meal), and meal is consumed by Then. The other orderings scramble producer/consumer roles. (BDD p.14-16)

questions.stem: In the state-transfer diagram, in which direction does state flow across the Given/When/Then bands?

options.text: Sideways within a single stage only

questions.explanation: The diagram shows state flowing downward from Given to When to Then. It is not upward, intra-stage-only, or random. (BDD p.12)

questions.stem: Where in the eight-phase change process does this lecture's BDD tooling primarily apply?
questions.explanation: BDD scenarios are the executable acceptance criteria of the Verification phase. Concept Location and Impact Analysis are earlier phases; Initiation only raises the request. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: Within a scenario, what is the correct execution order of the three clauses?
questions.explanation: Scenarios run Given (context) -> When (action) -> Then (assertion). Any other order breaks the pre-condition/action/outcome logic. (BDD p.5)

questions.stem: How does BDD connect the Verification phase to the Conclusion/CI phase in the change process?

options.text: Verification must finish before any code is written
JGiven's Maven/Jenkins plugins run the scenarios automatically on every integration
CI replaces the need for scenarios

questions.explanation: JGiven's CI plugins execute scenarios automatically at integration, linking Verification to Conclusion/CI. CI does not replace scenarios, and the phase order is not reversed. (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: Looking at a JGiven HTML5 report, which condition tells you the Verification phase has actually passed?

options.text: When at least half the scenarios pass
When the report is generated, regardless of results
When all scenarios are green and the report shows 0 Failed / 0 Pending
When a manual sign-off is added to the prose

questions.explanation: Verification passes when every scenario is green (0 Failed, 0 Pending) in the report. A partial pass or mere generation does not satisfy it. (BDD p.18)

questions.stem: A developer is mid-Actualization, writing production code. What is the BDD scenario's job at this moment?

options.text: It is generated automatically from the finished code
It records a bug found after release
It is irrelevant until the next project
It is the pre-written target the new code must satisfy

questions.explanation: The scenario, written before coding, is the acceptance target Actualization aims to satisfy. It is not auto-generated from code, a post-release bug log, or irrelevant. (BDDLab p.1)

questions.stem: Trace the deck's three movements in order. Which sequence is right?
questions.explanation: The deck moves from motivating/defining BDD (p.3-5), to JGiven (p.6-20), to AssertJ (p.21-30). The other orderings reshuffle these blocks. (BDD p.3-30)

questions.stem: Where does the When stage's 'some action' sit in the state pipeline relative to producing a result?

options.text: The action runs in Given before any state exists
The action runs in When and produces the result consumed by Then
The action runs in Then after the result exists

questions.explanation: Per the diagram, the When stage performs the action that produces the result, which Then then consumes. The action is not in Then/Given and does produce state. (BDD p.12)

questions.stem: In the lab's process, at which point is the Given/When/Then scenario created relative to coding?

options.text: Before coding — the story is turned into GWT first, so the acceptance criterion exists up front
It is never created; tests are ad hoc

questions.explanation: The lab turns the user story into GWT before coding so the acceptance criterion exists up front. It is not post-ship, review-only, or skipped. (BDDLab p.1)

questions.stem: Applying BDD to a JHotDraw change, which artefact serves as the Verification-phase gate?
questions.explanation: For any JHotDraw change, the BDD scenarios are the Verification-phase gate. A class diagram, build file, or commit message are not acceptance criteria. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: For the JHotDraw rectangle-drag scenario, which tool plays the Then-assertion role on the new coordinates?

options.text: AssertJ-Swing alone with no assertion library

questions.explanation: AssertJ performs the coordinate assertion in the Then step; AssertJ-Swing drives the GUI but AssertJ asserts the value. Gherkin/Mockito are not the assertion engine here. (BDD p.25,29)

questions.stem: On a sprawling case study like JHotDraw, what about JGiven stage reuse makes it especially worthwhile?

options.text: A stage like GivenADrawing can be reused across many figure scenarios
Large apps cannot use stages at all

questions.explanation: On a big app, a reusable GivenADrawing stage serves many scenarios, amplifying JGiven's modularity benefit. Stages do not require .feature files or disable AssertJ. (BDD p.11)

questions.stem: A reviewer worries a BDD scenario for JHotDraw's Observer-based view refresh will break whenever the Observer's internal field layout changes. Why is this worry usually unfounded?

options.text: BDD freezes the Observer's private fields so they never change
BDD disables the Observer during tests
BDD asserts the observable outcome (the view refreshes), not the pattern's internal structure
BDD regenerates the Observer code on each run

questions.explanation: Because BDD checks observable behaviour rather than internal layout, refactoring the Observer's fields does not break the scenario — behaviour over implementation. The other options misdescribe BDD. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: Which Swing-specific risk in JHotDraw can AssertJ-Swing help verify?

options.text: Violations of the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) threading rules

questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing can test EDT threading-rule violations, relevant to a Swing GUI like JHotDraw. Heap, network and DB concerns are outside its scope. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: Map the lab calculator story 'As a calculator user, I want to add two numbers so I can do addition.' Which GWT is correct?

options.text: Given two numbers 500 & 500, When I add them, Then I get 1000
Given a calculator, Then add, When result appears
When two numbers, Given addition, Then nothing
Given the result 1000, When I subtract, Then I get 500

questions.explanation: The lab maps it to: Given two numbers 500 & 500 / When I add them / Then result 1000. The other options scramble the clause order or operation. (BDDLab p.1)

questions.stem: In the lab, a user story is written from whose perspective and in what template?

options.text: The user's: 'As a [user type], I want [goal] so that [reason].'

questions.explanation: User stories use the 'As a [user], I want [goal] so that [reason]' template from the user's perspective. The other forms are code/SQL, not user stories. (BDDLab p.1)

questions.stem: For verifying a JHotDraw figure move at the GUI level, what does AssertJ-Swing embed in the HTML report when an assertion fails?

options.text: A video recording of the session
The full Java stack as an image only
A screenshot of the failed GUI test

questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing embeds screenshots of failed GUI tests in HTML reports. It does not record video or attach a .feature file (JGiven has none). (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: Applying the lab recipe to a Swing drawing app, which exact tool does the brief say to use to automate the scenarios?
questions.explanation: The lab states: for Swing applications use AssertJ-Swing to automate the scenarios. Selenium is for web, and Cucumber/Robot are classical text frameworks. (BDDLab p.1)

questions.stem: What does the JGiven HTML5 report become for a set of verified JHotDraw behaviours?

options.text: Living documentation readable by a domain expert
A UML diagram only developers can read
A binary the expert must decompile

questions.explanation: The report is expert-readable living documentation of the verified behaviours (though only developers can author the scenarios). It is not a binary, .feature, or developer-only UML. (BDD p.18)

questions.stem: Interpret: 'public GivenIngredients the_ingredient(String ingredient){ ingredients.add(ingredient); return this; }'. What two things does this step do?

options.text: Reads consumed state and deletes it
Records the ingredient and returns the stage for chaining
Asserts the ingredient and prints it
Injects a Spring bean and returns void

questions.explanation: It adds the ingredient to the provided list and returns 'this' for fluent chaining. It neither asserts/prints, nor injects, nor deletes state. (BDD p.14)

questions.stem: Interpret the When step body: 'assertThat(cook).isNotNull(); assertThat(dough).isNotNull(); meal = cook.fryDoughInAPan(dough); return this;'. What is the role of the two assertThat calls?

options.text: Sanity guards that required state is present before the action

questions.explanation: The two assertThat(...).isNotNull() calls guard that the collaborator and dough exist before frying — sanity checks, not the final Then assertion. (BDD p.15)

questions.stem: Interpret 'public void the_resulting_meal_is_a(String expectedMeal){ assertThat(meal).isEqualTo(expectedMeal); }'. Which clause and library?

options.text: A When step using JUnit assertEquals
A Then step using AssertJ's isEqualTo

questions.explanation: Asserting the produced meal with AssertJ's isEqualTo is the Then step. It is not Given/When, nor Hamcrest/JUnit/Spring. (BDD p.16)

questions.stem: Interpret 'assertThat(actualArray).contains("is").startsWith("This");' where actualArray is a String[]. What does it demonstrate?

options.text: Polymorphism — the same methods work on String and String[] via type-specific asserts
That contains() is a JUnit method
A compile error because arrays lack these methods

questions.explanation: The same contains()/startsWith() names work on both String and String[] because assertThat returns the right type-specific assert — polymorphism. It compiles and is AssertJ, not JUnit. (BDD p.25)

questions.stem: Interpret the Condition example: 'assertThat(12).is(evenDivBySix); assertThat(8).isNot(evenDivBySix);'. What is being checked?

options.text: 12 satisfies the divisible-by-six predicate; 8 does not

questions.explanation: is(condition) checks 12 % 6 == 0 (true), isNot(condition) checks 8 is not divisible by six (true). It is not an equality or array-contains check. (BDD p.27)

questions.stem: Interpret 'public GivenIngredients an_egg(){ return the_ingredient("egg"); }'. What pattern is shown?

options.text: A convenience step delegating to a parameterised step, staying chainable

questions.explanation: an_egg() delegates to the_ingredient("egg") and returns the stage, a convenience-step-over-parameterised-step pattern that keeps chaining. It is not an assertion, teardown, or static factory. (BDD p.14)

questions.stem: Interpret the class header 'public class ThenMeal extends Stage<ThenMeal>'. What does it tell you?

options.text: ThenMeal is a Spring configuration class
ThenMeal is a Cucumber step-definition file
ThenMeal is an AssertJ assertion class
ThenMeal is a JGiven stage whose self-type is itself

questions.explanation: Extending Stage<ThenMeal> marks it as a JGiven stage with itself as the self-type for chaining. It is not AssertJ, Spring config, or a Cucumber file. (BDD p.11,16)

questions.stem: Interpret the field set in WhenCook: '@ExpectedScenarioState List<String> ingredients;' with no initializer. Why no 'new ArrayList<>()'?

options.text: The value is injected from the Given stage that provided it, so no local init is needed
Because the list is always empty
Because ingredients is a static constant

questions.explanation: An @ExpectedScenarioState field receives its value from the upstream provider, so a local initializer is unnecessary. It is not always empty, forbidden, or static. (BDD p.15)

questions.stem: Interpret the console fragment 'Then the resulting meal is a pan cake'. What is this line, in JGiven terms?

options.text: The rendered Then step of the executed scenario (living documentation)

questions.explanation: It is the executed Then step rendered as readable prose in JGiven's console report — living documentation. It is not a warning, Gherkin source, or diff. (BDD p.17)

questions.stem: Interpret the custom-assertion goal 'assertThat(student).isInMiddleSchool()'. What must exist for this to compile?

options.text: A Gherkin step matching the sentence
A StudentAssert (extends AbstractAssert) with isInMiddleSchool() and an Assertions subclass providing the factory

questions.explanation: It needs a custom AbstractAssert subclass with the method plus an Assertions factory returning it. A Condition gives is()/isNot(), not a named method; Gherkin/String are irrelevant. (BDD p.28)

questions.stem: You start a JGiven scenario method and call given() first. What does given() return so you can chain .an_egg()?
questions.explanation: given() returns the Given stage so step methods chain on it. It does not return a boolean, void, or a parser. (BDD p.10)

questions.stem: You must add a second Given pre-condition 'some milk' after 'an egg' in the scenario method. Which call expresses the chaining 'And'?
questions.explanation: Extra Given clauses chain with and(), e.g. given().an_egg().and().some_milk(). or()/with() are not JGiven chain methods, and then() would start the assertion clause. (BDD p.10)

questions.stem: Which is NOT part of the deck's central JGiven trade-off statement?

options.text: JGiven lets experts author scenarios more easily than Cucumber
JGiven buys low maintenance by living in Java

questions.explanation: The trade-off is exactly that experts CANNOT author JGiven scenarios (unlike plain-text Cucumber). The other three accurately state the trade-off's parts. (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: Contrast the data-passing mechanism of JGiven stages with ordinary method parameters.

options.text: JGiven serialises state to disk between stages
JGiven uses a global mutable singleton
JGiven uses annotated fields auto-copied between stages, not explicit parameters
JGiven passes everything as method parameters

questions.explanation: State moves via annotated fields that JGiven copies between same-typed fields of consecutive stages — not explicit parameters, a global singleton, or disk. (BDD p.13)

questions.stem: Because the report text is generated from class/method/step names, what coding habit most improves report readability?

options.text: Naming step methods in clear snake_case prose

questions.explanation: Since JGiven renders names into the report, descriptive snake_case step names yield readable living documentation. Single letters, XML comments and compiler flags do not help. (BDD p.10,17)

questions.stem: To gain modularity and reuse in JGiven scenarios, you should organise steps into what?
questions.explanation: Stage classes provide the modularity and reuse. A single giant method, Gherkin features, or loose statics do not give JGiven's stage reuse. (BDD p.11)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'AssertJ contains() and startsWith() are different APIs for String and array.' Correct it.

options.text: They are the same polymorphic methods; assertThat returns the right type-specific assert
They are different APIs requiring separate imports

questions.explanation: The same method names work on String and array via the type-specific assert returned by assertThat — polymorphism, not separate APIs. Arrays do support them. (BDD p.25)

questions.stem: Put the artefacts in lifecycle order for one behaviour: (1) user story, (2) GWT scenario, (3) JGiven Java test, (4) green HTML report.
questions.explanation: A story becomes a GWT scenario, then a JGiven Java test, which when run yields the green report. The other orders break the dependency chain. (BDDLab p.1; BDD p.10,18)

questions.stem: A JHotDraw Observer should refresh the view when a figure moves. How would a BDD scenario verify this without coupling to the pattern internals?

options.text: Reflectively inspect the notify() call stack
Assert the Observer's private listener list size
Given a figure with an attached view, When it moves, Then the view shows the new position

questions.explanation: BDD checks the observable outcome (the view updates) rather than internal structure like private lists or bytecode. That is behaviour-over-implementation. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: Interpret the absence of any '.feature' file in a JGiven project. What does it imply about maintenance?

options.text: Only one artefact (Java) must be maintained, lowering sync cost
Two artefacts must still be synced

questions.explanation: No .feature file means a single Java artefact, removing the classical sync cost. Scenarios still run and reports still generate. (BDD p.10,20)

questions.stem: In BDD, the 'Then' clause is implemented in JGiven primarily with which library?
questions.explanation: Then steps assert with AssertJ (e.g. assertThat(meal).isEqualTo(...)). Mockito mocks, Log4j logs, Gson parses JSON. (BDD p.16,24)

questions.stem: Why is collaboration between developers and domain experts a defining BDD property rather than a nice-to-have?

options.text: Because it speeds up the JVM
Because experts must compile the tests
Shared definition of behaviour in common language is what makes the spec meaningful to all roles
Because developers cannot write code alone

questions.explanation: Collaboration ensures behaviour is jointly defined in shared language, making the executable spec meaningful to developers, testers, and experts. It is not about compiling or JVM speed. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: AssertJ's assertThat() is described as a what?
questions.explanation: assertThat() is a type-specific factory returning the right assert per value type. It is not a mock, logger, or build plugin. (BDD p.24)

questions.stem: Cause->consequence: a developer marks the Then stage's meal field @ProvidedScenarioState instead of @ExpectedScenarioState. Likely result?

options.text: The Then stage will not receive the meal value it needs to assert
The HTML report becomes plain text

questions.explanation: Marking a consumed field as Provided means the Then stage does not import the upstream meal, so the assertion lacks its value. Speed, Spring cycles and report format are unaffected. (BDD p.13,16)

questions.stem: Code interpretation: 'Condition<Integer> evenDivBySix = new Condition<>(){ boolean matches(Integer v){ return v%6==0; } };'. What kind of object is evenDivBySix?
questions.explanation: A Condition overriding matches() is a reusable predicate functor, applied via is()/isNot(). It is not an assertion class, stage, or bean. (BDD p.27)

questions.stem: Which keyword chains an extra action under When?
questions.explanation: Both Given and When clauses chain extra steps with And (e.g. When ... And the cook fries the dough). Or/Plus/Next are not GWT keywords. (BDD p.5)

questions.stem: Misconception-buster: 'Provided=in, Expected=out.' What is the correct mapping?
questions.explanation: Correct mnemonic: Provided = out (producer), Expected = in (consumer). The stated mapping is reversed. (BDD p.13)

questions.stem: Applied/cause: if a JHotDraw GUI test mutates a figure off the EDT, what can AssertJ-Swing reveal?
questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing can surface EDT threading-rule violations. Leaks, SQL injection and merge conflicts are unrelated to its checks. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: Which is the action clause keyword in GWT?
questions.explanation: When marks the action/event. Given is the context, Then the outcome, And just chains clauses. (BDD p.5)

questions.stem: Cause->consequence: because stage methods can be reused across scenarios, what large-project benefit did TNG observe?

options.text: Greatly improved readability and reusability of test code

questions.explanation: TNG reported greatly improved readability and reusability with JGiven (and easy onboarding) — the opposite of the wrong options. (BDD p.19)

questions.stem: Which best describes 'living documentation'?

options.text: Documentation that is the executing test, so it cannot silently go stale

questions.explanation: Living documentation is the executing scenario itself, kept current because drift makes it fail. It is not a manual, stale wiki, or UML export. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: In JGiven, one concrete Given/When/Then behaviour maps to which code unit?
questions.explanation: A single GWT behaviour (a scenario) is one JUnit test method in JGiven. A stage holds shared steps, a Condition is a predicate, a Maven module is a build unit. (BDD p.10)

questions.stem: AssertJ chained calls like .contains().startsWith() are an example of what API style?
questions.explanation: Chained, sentence-like calls are a fluent API. They are not reflection, callbacks, or I/O streaming. (BDD p.24-25)

questions.stem: What does a JGiven stage class typically correspond to?

options.text: One of the Given, When, or Then steps

questions.explanation: Typically one stage per Given/When/Then step. Not per assertion line, per project, or per annotation. (BDD p.11)

questions.stem: In JGiven, what plays the role that a Gherkin .feature file plays in Cucumber?

options.text: The Java test method and stage classes

questions.explanation: JGiven's Java test method plus stage classes carry the scenario, replacing Cucumber's .feature file. It is not XML, Groovy, or YAML. (BDD p.10)

questions.stem: AssertJ is primarily used inside which BDD scenario clause?
questions.explanation: AssertJ performs assertions in the Then clause (with sanity checks possible in When). It is not Given-, When-only, or And-only. (BDD p.16,24)

questions.stem: Which AssertJ method asserts exact equality in the pancake Then step?
questions.explanation: ThenMeal uses assertThat(meal).isEqualTo("pancake"). contains/startsWith are substring/prefix checks and is() applies a Condition. (BDD p.16)

questions.stem: Which best names the practice of writing behaviour first in shared language, then automating it as tests?
questions.explanation: That practice is Behaviour-Driven Development. CI integrates code, Impact Analysis scopes change, and Refactoring restructures code. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: What is the running example dish used throughout the JGiven part of the deck?
questions.explanation: The deck's running example is the pancake scenario. It is not pizza, salad, or soup. (BDD p.5)

questions.stem: Which keyword opens the pre-condition clause of a BDD scenario?
questions.explanation: Given opens the pre-condition/context clause. When is the action, Then the outcome, And just chains. (BDD p.5)

questions.stem: Reverse-id: a class extends Stage<...> and holds snake_case step methods. What is it?
questions.explanation: Extending Stage<...> with snake_case steps is a JGiven stage class. It is not a Condition, .feature file, or Spring config. (BDD p.11)

questions.stem: Which annotation marks a stage field that is purely consumed from an earlier stage?
questions.explanation: @ExpectedScenarioState is the consumer (in) annotation. Provided is producer, Autowired is DI, @Test marks a test method. (BDD p.13)

questions.stem: Which library provides the fluent assertThat used in Then steps?
questions.explanation: AssertJ provides the fluent assertThat factory for Then steps. Mockito mocks, Log4j logs, and assertEquals is JUnit's non-fluent assert. (BDD p.24)

questions.stem: AssertJ-Swing is specifically for testing what kind of application?
questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing automates Swing GUI tests. CLI jobs, REST services and DB procedures are outside its scope. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: Which framework does the deck pick as its developer-friendly example to drill into?
questions.explanation: The deck commits to JGiven for the BDD section. Cucumber, Fitnesse and Concordion are classical examples it contrasts against. (BDD p.7-8)

questions.stem: Negative: which is NOT a Given/When/Then continuation keyword?
questions.explanation: Clauses chain with And; 'But-Maybe' is not a GWT keyword. Given and Then are clause openers. (BDD p.5)

questions.stem: At which change-process phase do BDD scenarios act as acceptance criteria?
questions.explanation: BDD scenarios are the executable acceptance criteria of the Verification phase. The others are earlier phases. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: Which AssertJ feature is a reusable predicate applied via is()/isNot()?
questions.explanation: A Condition is the reusable predicate used with is()/isNot(). A custom assertion adds named methods; assertThat is the factory; Swing is GUI testing. (BDD p.27)

questions.stem: What does JGiven generate that a domain expert can read as living documentation?
questions.explanation: JGiven generates console and HTML5 reports that serve as expert-readable living documentation. Class files, poms and diffs are not that. (BDD p.17-18)
